title: Software Development Life Cycle Models
description: Compare SDLC methodologies including Waterfall, Agile, and DevOps

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Different SDLC methodologies have different risk profiles and control points. Auditors must understand the methodology to identify appropriate controls.

sections.title: Traditional Waterfall Model
sections.content:
**Phases (Sequential):**

**1. Requirements:**
• Gather and document requirements
• Sign-off before proceeding

**2. Design:**
• System architecture
• Detailed specifications

**3. Development:**
• Code the solution
• Unit testing

**4. Testing:**
• Integration testing
• System testing
• User acceptance testing

**5. Deployment:**
• Implementation
• Production release

**6. Maintenance:**
• Ongoing support
• Enhancements

**Characteristics:**
• Sequential phases
• Heavy documentation
• Formal sign-offs between phases
• Changes are costly
• Best for stable, well-understood requirements

sections.title: Agile Principles
sections.content:
**Agile Manifesto Values:**

• **Individuals and interactions** over processes and tools
• **Working software** over comprehensive documentation
• **Customer collaboration** over contract negotiation
• **Responding to change** over following a plan

**Popular Agile Frameworks:**

**Scrum:**
• Fixed-length sprints (2-4 weeks)
• Roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Team
• Ceremonies: Sprint planning, daily standup, review, retrospective
• Artifacts: Product backlog, sprint backlog, increment

**Kanban:**
• Continuous flow (no fixed iterations)
• Visualize workflow
• Limit work in progress (WIP)
• Optimize flow

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Scrum Ceremonies
sections.content:
**Scrum has 4 ceremonies: PSRR**

• **P**lanning - What will we build this sprint?
• **S**tandup (Daily) - What did I do? What will I do? Blockers?
• **R**eview - Demo the working software
• **R**etrospective - How can we improve?

Think: "Plan, Stand, Review, Reflect"

sections.title: DevOps and CI/CD
sections.content:
**DevOps = Development + Operations**

Cultural and technical practices that unite development and operations.

**Key Practices:**

**Continuous Integration (CI):**
• Frequent code commits
• Automated builds
• Automated testing
• Fast feedback

**Continuous Delivery (CD):**
• Code always deployable
• Automated deployment pipeline
• Manual approval for production

**Continuous Deployment:**
• Fully automated to production
• No manual gates
• Requires mature automation

**Infrastructure as Code (IaC):**
• Infrastructure defined in code
• Version controlled
• Consistent environments
• Automated provisioning

sections.title: Choosing a Methodology
sections.content:
**Waterfall Best For:**
• Stable, well-understood requirements
• Regulatory requirements for documentation
• Fixed-price contracts
• Hardware-integrated systems

**Agile Best For:**
• Evolving requirements
• Need for rapid delivery
• Customer available for feedback
• Innovation projects

**Hybrid Approaches:**
• Combine elements of both
• Large programs with Agile teams
• Governance phases with Agile execution

**Audit Implications:**
• Methodology affects control points
• Agile requires different controls than Waterfall
• Controls must fit the approach used

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Waterfall is sequential with fixed requirements; Agile is iterative with evolving requirements
Agile values working software, customer collaboration, and responding to change
Scrum uses fixed sprints with defined ceremonies; Kanban uses continuous flow
DevOps unites Dev and Ops with CI/CD pipelines and Infrastructure as Code
Methodology choice affects risk profile and appropriate controls

knowledgeCheck.question: In a traditional Waterfall SDLC model, which of the following is the MOST critical activity to ensure successful project completion BEFORE proceeding to the Design phase?
options: Obtaining sign-off on the documented requirements.
Developing a high-level system architecture diagram.
Conducting a preliminary risk assessment.
Establishing a change management process.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: In the Waterfall model, each phase must be completed and approved before moving to the next. Sign-off on documented requirements is crucial because later phases depend heavily on these requirements. Changes later in the process are costly and disruptive.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the sequential and rigid nature of the Waterfall model.

reveal.question: What are the four values outlined in the Agile Manifesto?
reveal.answer:
1. Individuals and interactions over processes and tools.
2. Working software over comprehensive documentation.
3. Customer collaboration over contract negotiation.
4. Responding to change over following a plan.

rows.label: Approach
values: Sequential, phase-based
Iterative, incremental

rows.label: Change Management
values: Resistant to change; changes are costly
Embraces change; adaptable to new requirements

rows.label: Customer Involvement
values: Limited customer involvement after requirements gathering
Continuous customer collaboration

title: Requirements Management
description: Learn how to define, document, and manage system requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Poor requirements are the leading cause of project failure. Auditors must understand requirements management to evaluate project risk.

sections.title: Requirements Elicitation Techniques
sections.content:
**Common Techniques:**

**Interviews:**
• One-on-one discussions
• Good for detailed understanding
• Time-consuming for many stakeholders

**Workshops/JAD Sessions:**
• Joint Application Development
• Collaborative group sessions
• Efficient for consensus building

**Surveys/Questionnaires:**
• Collect from many stakeholders
• Quantifiable results
• May miss nuances

**Document Analysis:**
• Review existing documentation
• Understand current state
• Identify implicit requirements

**Observation:**
• Watch users perform tasks
• Understand real workflow
• Identify unstated needs

**Prototyping:**
• Build mockups for feedback
• Validate understanding
• Iterative refinement

sections.title: SMART Requirements
sections.content:
**Good Requirements Are SMART:**

**S - Specific:**
• Clear and unambiguous
• One interpretation only

**M - Measurable:**
• Criteria for verification
• Can be tested

**A - Achievable:**
• Technically feasible
• Within project constraints

**R - Relevant:**
• Supports business objectives
• Necessary for the system

**T - Time-bound:**
• When it's needed
• Priority relative to others

**Example:**
❌ "The system should be fast"
✅ "The search results page shall load within 2 seconds for 95% of requests under normal load conditions"

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Requirements Quality
sections.content:
**Check requirements with CURT:**

• **C**omplete - Nothing missing?
• **U**nambiguous - Only one interpretation?
• **R**ealizable - Can we build it?
• **T**estable - Can we verify it?

"Be CURT with bad requirements!"

sections.title: Requirements Traceability
sections.content:
**Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM):**

Links requirements through the entire lifecycle:

Business Need → Requirement → Design → Code → Test → Implementation

**Benefits:**
• Verify all requirements implemented
• Impact analysis for changes
• Test coverage confirmation
• Audit trail

**Traceability Questions:**
• Forward: Is each requirement implemented and tested?
• Backward: Does each feature trace to a requirement?
• Coverage: Are test cases covering all requirements?

**Audit Consideration:**
Ask to see the RTM; verify it's maintained and used.

sections.title: Managing Requirements Changes
sections.content:
**Change Control for Requirements:**

**1. Request:**
• Document the change request
• Identify requester and rationale

**2. Analyze:**
• Assess impact on scope, schedule, cost
• Evaluate technical feasibility
• Identify affected components

**3. Approve:**
• Appropriate authority decides
• Document decision
• Update baselines

**4. Implement:**
• Make the change
• Update all related artifacts
• Verify implementation

**Scope Creep:**
Uncontrolled expansion of requirements.
Prevented by: Change control, baseline management, governance.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Requirements include functional, non-functional, business, user, system, and regulatory
Elicitation techniques: interviews, workshops, surveys, document analysis, observation, prototyping
Good requirements are SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
Traceability links requirements to design, code, and tests throughout the lifecycle
Requirements changes must be controlled to prevent scope creep

knowledgeCheck.question: Which requirements elicitation technique is MOST suitable when a large number of stakeholders need to provide input efficiently, but detailed individual understanding is less critical?
options: Interviews
Workshops/JAD Sessions
Surveys/Questionnaires
Prototyping
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Surveys/Questionnaires are the most efficient method for gathering input from a large number of stakeholders. Interviews are time-consuming, and workshops, while collaborative, may not be feasible with a very large group. Prototyping is not explicitly covered in the lesson, but also not efficient for the initial gathering of requirements from a large group.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the trade-off between depth of understanding and the number of stakeholders involved.

reveal.question: What does the 'R' in the SMART requirements acronym stand for, and why is it important?
reveal.answer: The 'R' stands for 'Relevant'. A requirement is relevant if it supports the business objectives. Irrelevant requirements add unnecessary complexity and cost, and may detract from the overall goal of the project.

rows.label: Stakeholder Involvement
values: One-on-one
Collaborative group

rows.label: Time Efficiency
values: Time-consuming for many stakeholders
Efficient for consensus building

rows.label: Depth of Understanding
values: Good for detailed understanding
Suitable for general agreement and brainstorming

title: Application Testing
description: Master testing methodologies, levels, and quality assurance practices

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Testing verifies that systems work correctly and securely. Auditors must understand testing to evaluate whether quality controls are adequate.

sections.title: Testing Types
sections.content:
**Functional Testing:**
• Verify features work as specified
• Positive tests (happy path)
• Negative tests (error handling)

**Non-Functional Testing:**

**Performance Testing:**
• Load testing (normal conditions)
• Stress testing (beyond normal)
• Scalability testing (growth capacity)

**Security Testing:**
• Vulnerability scanning
• Penetration testing
• Code analysis (SAST/DAST)

**Usability Testing:**
• User experience evaluation
• Accessibility testing

**Compatibility Testing:**
• Browser/device compatibility
• Operating system compatibility

**Recovery Testing:**
• Failover testing
• Backup/restore verification

sections.title: Black Box vs White Box Testing
sections.content:
**Black Box Testing:**
• Test without seeing internal code
• Focus on inputs and outputs
• Based on requirements/specifications
• Performed by QA, users

**White Box Testing:**
• Test with knowledge of code
• Focus on internal logic
• Code coverage analysis
• Performed by developers

**Gray Box Testing:**
• Partial knowledge of internals
• Combination approach
• Common for integration testing

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Testing Colors
sections.content:
**BLACK Box = Blind to code**
You can't see inside; test from outside

**WHITE Box = Window into code**
You can see everything; test the logic

**GRAY Box = Glasses on**
You can see some things; combination

sections.title: Test Documentation
sections.content:
**Test Plan:**
• Strategy and approach
• Scope and objectives
• Resources and schedule
• Entry and exit criteria
• Risk assessment

**Test Cases:**
• Unique identifier
• Preconditions
• Test steps
• Expected results
• Actual results
• Pass/Fail status

**Test Scripts:**
• Automated test code
• Reusable across cycles
• Version controlled

**Defect Reports:**
• Description and steps to reproduce
• Severity and priority
• Screenshots/evidence
• Status tracking

sections.title: User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
sections.content:
**Purpose:**
Business validation that the system meets their needs.

**Critical Success Factors:**

**Business Ownership:**
• Business users execute tests
• IT supports, doesn't perform UAT
• Sign-off demonstrates acceptance

**Representative Testing:**
• Realistic test data
• Real-world scenarios
• Include exception cases

**Independence:**
• Separate from development environment
• Production-like configuration

**Acceptance Criteria:**
• Pre-defined criteria for success
• Documented before testing starts
• Linked to requirements

**Audit Tip:** Always ask who performed UAT and whether business sign-off was obtained.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Test levels: Unit → Integration → System → UAT (increasing scope)
Test types include functional, performance, security, usability, compatibility
Black box tests without code knowledge; White box tests with code visibility
Test documentation: Plan, Cases, Scripts, Defect reports
UAT must be performed by business users with documented acceptance criteria

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following testing types is PRIMARILY concerned with verifying that the 'Forgot Password' feature in a web application functions as designed, including handling invalid email addresses?
options: Performance Testing
Functional Testing
Security Testing
Scalability Testing
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Functional testing focuses on verifying that specific features of an application work as intended, covering both positive (happy path) and negative (error handling) scenarios. The 'Forgot Password' feature and its associated error handling directly relate to its functionality.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what aspect of the application is being directly evaluated: its features or its attributes.

reveal.question: Define 'Stress Testing' and explain its purpose.
reveal.answer: Stress testing involves subjecting a system to conditions beyond its normal operating capacity. Its purpose is to identify the breaking point of the system, determine its stability under extreme loads, and uncover vulnerabilities that may not be apparent under normal conditions. This helps ensure the system can handle unexpected spikes in demand or resource consumption.

sections.title: Black Box vs. White Box Testing

rows.label: Knowledge of Code
values: No knowledge of internal code required
Requires knowledge of internal code

rows.label: Focus
values: Focuses on inputs and outputs
Focuses on internal logic and code coverage

rows.label: Tester Profile
values: Typically performed by QA engineers or end-users
Typically performed by developers

title: Change and Release Management
description: Understand controls for managing changes to production systems

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Uncontrolled changes are a leading cause of outages and security incidents. Change management is one of the most audited IT controls.

sections.title: Change Management Process
sections.content:
**1. Request:**
• Submit change request
• Document business justification
• Identify affected systems

**2. Assess:**
• Evaluate impact and risk
• Resource requirements
• Dependencies

**3. Approve:**
• Change Advisory Board (CAB) review
• Appropriate authority approval
• Schedule assignment

**4. Build:**
• Develop and configure change
• Prepare documentation
• Create back-out plan

**5. Test:**
• Test in non-production
• Verify functionality
• UAT where appropriate

**6. Implement:**
• Execute per schedule
• Monitor for issues
• Validate success

**7. Review:**
• Post-implementation review
• Close change ticket
• Lessons learned

sections.title: Change Advisory Board (CAB)
sections.content:
**Purpose:**
Review and authorize changes to minimize risk.

**Typical Composition:**
• Change manager (chair)
• Technical representatives
• Operations/support
• Security representative
• Business representatives (as needed)

**CAB Reviews:**
• Risk and impact assessment
• Resource availability
• Scheduling conflicts
• Back-out plans
• Test results

**ECAB (Emergency CAB):**
• Subset available for emergency changes
• Smaller group for quick decisions
• Still provides oversight

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Change Control
sections.content:
**Change Request needs: RABBIT**

• **R**eason for change
• **A**ffected systems
• **B**ack-out plan
• **B**usiness sign-off
• **I**mpact assessment
• **T**esting evidence

Every change needs a RABBIT to survive!

sections.title: Segregation of Duties in Changes
sections.content:
**Key Separations:**

**Developer ≠ Production Access:**
• Developers code in dev/test
• Separate team deploys to production
• Prevents unauthorized changes

**Requestor ≠ Approver:**
• Cannot approve own changes
• Independent review required

**Developer ≠ Tester (for critical systems):**
• Independent testing
• Unbiased validation

**Audit Focus:**
• Review who can access production
• Verify approval is independent
• Check for segregation violations
• Sample emergency changes for compliance

sections.title: Emergency Change Controls
sections.content:
**Emergency Change Requirements:**

**During Emergency:**
• Verbal authorization acceptable
• Document who authorized
• Implement fix
• Basic testing if possible

**After Emergency:**
• Create retrospective change ticket
• Document what was done
• CAB post-review
• Root cause analysis
• Lessons learned

**Red Flags:**
• High volume of emergency changes
• Emergency changes not documented
• No post-review performed
• Emergency used to bypass controls

sections.title: Release Management
sections.content:
**Release vs. Change:**
• Change = Individual modification
• Release = Bundle of changes deployed together

**Release Process:**
• Package multiple changes
• Coordinated testing
• Scheduled deployment windows
• Communication plan
• Rollback strategy

**Release Types:**
• Major Release: Significant new functionality
• Minor Release: Enhancements and fixes
• Patch: Urgent fixes between releases

**Environments:**
Development → Test → Staging → Production
Each has its own controls and access restrictions.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Change process: Request → Assess → Approve → Build → Test → Implement → Review
Change types: Standard (pre-approved), Normal (full process), Emergency (expedited)
CAB reviews changes for risk, impact, and scheduling
Segregation of duties: Developers shouldn't have production access or approve their own changes
Emergency changes require post-implementation review and documentation

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST important reason for documenting the business justification during the 'Request' phase of the change management process?
options: To ensure the change is technically feasible.
To provide a clear understanding of the change's objectives and benefits, enabling informed decision-making.
To identify all affected systems and components.
To streamline the change approval process.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Documenting the business justification provides the context for evaluating the change's value and alignment with organizational goals. While technical feasibility and affected systems are important, the business justification drives the decision to proceed or not. Streamlining the process is a secondary benefit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the overall goals of change management and why changes are even proposed in the first place.

reveal.question: Name four typical members of a Change Advisory Board (CAB).
reveal.answer: Typical CAB members include: Change Manager (chair), Technical Representatives, Operations/Support Representatives, Security Representative, and Business Representatives (as needed).

rows.label: Focus
values: Managing individual changes to IT infrastructure and applications.
Planning, scheduling, and controlling the movement of software changes into production environments.

rows.label: Primary Goal
values: Minimize disruption and risk associated with changes.
Ensure smooth and efficient deployment of releases with minimal impact on services.

rows.label: Activities
values: Request submission, impact assessment, approval, implementation, and review.
Release planning, build management, testing, deployment, and post-implementation review.

title: Project Management Fundamentals
description: Learn project management principles and audit considerations for IT projects

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: IT projects frequently fail or exceed budgets. Understanding project management helps auditors identify and escalate project risks.

sections.title: Project Management Knowledge Areas
sections.content:
**PMBOK 10 Knowledge Areas:**

1. **Integration Management**: Coordinate all elements
2. **Scope Management**: Define and control what's included
3. **Schedule Management**: Plan and control timeline
4. **Cost Management**: Estimate, budget, control costs
5. **Quality Management**: Ensure quality requirements met
6. **Resource Management**: Acquire and manage team
7. **Communications Management**: Plan and manage communications
8. **Risk Management**: Identify and respond to risks
9. **Procurement Management**: Acquire goods and services
10. **Stakeholder Management**: Engage stakeholders

sections.title: Triple Constraint
sections.content:
**The Iron Triangle:**

Scope, Schedule, Cost are interconnected.

• Increase Scope → Need more Time or Money
• Reduce Schedule → Need to cut Scope or add Resources (Cost)
• Reduce Cost → Need to cut Scope or extend Schedule

**Quality** is often placed in the center - affected by trade-offs in the triangle.

**Audit Perspective:**
• Are constraints realistic?
• Are trade-offs documented and approved?
• Is quality being sacrificed?

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Project Risks
sections.content:
**Common project risks follow SCOPE:**

• **S**chedule risk - Will it be on time?
• **C**ost risk - Will it be on budget?
• **O**perational risk - Will it work in production?
• **P**eople risk - Do we have the right skills?
• **E**xternal risk - Vendor, regulatory, market

sections.title: Project Risk Management
sections.content:
**Risk Management Process:**

**1. Identify Risks:**
• Brainstorming
• Checklists
• Expert judgment
• Requirements review

**2. Analyze Risks:**
• Probability assessment
• Impact assessment
• Risk ranking/prioritization

**3. Plan Responses:**
• Avoid: Eliminate the risk
• Mitigate: Reduce likelihood/impact
• Transfer: Shift to third party
• Accept: Acknowledge and proceed

**4. Monitor Risks:**
• Track identified risks
• Watch for new risks
• Execute responses when triggered

sections.title: Project Governance
sections.content:
**Governance Elements:**

**Project Steering Committee:**
• Executive oversight
• Major decision authority
• Escalation point
• Resource prioritization

**Project Sponsor:**
• Executive accountable for success
• Provides resources and authority
• Removes barriers
• Champions the project

**Project Manager:**
• Day-to-day management
• Manages team and tasks
• Reports status
• Manages risks and issues

**Gates/Stage Reviews:**
• Milestones requiring approval
• Go/no-go decisions
• Quality gates

sections.title: Common Project Failure Indicators
sections.content:
**Red Flags for Auditors:**

**Scope:**
• Unclear or changing requirements
• Scope creep without change control
• No documented scope statement

**Schedule:**
• Unrealistic timeline
• Consistently missed milestones
• No contingency buffer

**Resources:**
• Key resources not available
• High team turnover
• Skill gaps

**Governance:**
• No executive sponsorship
• Infrequent steering meetings
• Issues not escalated

**Communication:**
• Stakeholders surprised
• Status reports not accurate
• Problems hidden

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: PMBOK defines 10 knowledge areas covering all aspects of project management
Project phases: Initiation → Planning → Execution → Monitoring → Closing
Triple constraint: Scope, Schedule, and Cost are interdependent trade-offs
Risk responses: Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, Accept
Strong governance requires steering committee, sponsor, PM, and stage gates

knowledgeCheck.question: During a project, the project sponsor mandates a significant reduction in the project budget. According to the 'Iron Triangle' principle, what is the MOST likely consequence?
options: The project schedule will be shortened without impacting scope or quality.
The project scope will need to be reduced or the project schedule extended to maintain quality.
The project team will need to work overtime to compensate for the budget reduction, maintaining scope and schedule.
The project's risk management plan will need to be updated to reflect the increased financial risk, but scope and schedule will remain unchanged.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The 'Iron Triangle' (Scope, Schedule, Cost) highlights the interconnectedness of these constraints. Reducing cost (budget) typically necessitates a trade-off in either scope (reducing what's delivered) or schedule (extending the timeline) to maintain quality. Options A, C and D are unrealistic given the constraints of the Iron Triangle.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the direct relationships between cost, scope, and schedule.

reveal.question: Name the 10 Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK) Knowledge Areas.
reveal.answer: 1. Integration Management, 2. Scope Management, 3. Schedule Management, 4. Cost Management, 5. Quality Management, 6. Resource Management, 7. Communications Management, 8. Risk Management, 9. Procurement Management, 10. Stakeholder Management

knowledgeCheck.question: Which project management knowledge area is MOST concerned with coordinating all other knowledge areas and processes throughout the project lifecycle?
options: Project Scope Management
Project Risk Management
Project Integration Management
Project Communications Management
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Project Integration Management is the overarching knowledge area focused on coordinating and unifying all other project management processes. It ensures that the various elements of the project are properly aligned and working together to achieve the project objectives. The other options focus on specific aspects of the project, rather than the overall coordination.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which area is the 'glue' that holds everything together.

title: Configuration Management
description: Understand configuration management and version control practices

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Configuration management ensures systems are in a known, approved state. It's essential for change impact analysis, troubleshooting, and security.

sections.title: Configuration Management Concepts
sections.content:
**Configuration Item (CI):**
Any component that needs to be managed:
• Hardware (servers, network devices)
• Software (applications, operating systems)
• Documentation (policies, procedures)
• Services

**Configuration Baseline:**
• Approved state at a point in time
• Reference for changes
• Enables rollback

**Configuration Management Database (CMDB):**
• Repository of all CIs
• Attributes and relationships
• Change history
• Enables impact analysis

sections.title: CMDB Structure
rows: App runs on Server, DB supports App

sections.title: Configuration Management Process
sections.content:
**1. Identification:**
• Define what CIs to track
• Naming conventions
• Categorization scheme

**2. Control:**
• Only authorized changes
• Change management integration
• Version control

**3. Status Accounting:**
• Track current state of all CIs
• Report changes
• Maintain history

**4. Verification and Audit:**
• Compare actual to CMDB
• Identify discrepancies
• Ensure accuracy

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Configuration Management
sections.content:
**ICSA Process:**

• **I**dentify what to track
• **C**ontrol changes to it
• **S**tatus accounting (track state)
• **A**udit for accuracy

Think: "I C Servers Always" - I always see what's configured!

sections.title: Version Control
sections.content:
**Version Control Systems:**
• Track changes to code/documents
• Maintain history
• Enable collaboration
• Support rollback

**Key Features:**
• Check-in/check-out
• Branching and merging
• Conflict resolution
• Audit trail

**Popular Tools:**
• Git (most common)
• Subversion (SVN)
• Perforce
• Team Foundation Server

**Best Practices:**
• Frequent commits with meaningful messages
• Branch for features/releases
• Code review before merge
• Protect main/master branch

sections.title: Auditing Configuration Management
sections.content:
**Audit Areas:**

**CMDB Accuracy:**
• Is the CMDB current?
• Do physical audits match CMDB?
• Are discrepancies investigated?

**Change Control:**
• Are all changes reflected in CMDB?
• Is CMDB updated as part of change process?
• Is history maintained?

**Version Control:**
• Is all code in version control?
• Are access controls appropriate?
• Is the audit trail complete?
• Can any version be reconstructed?

**Baselines:**
• Are baselines established?
• Can systems be compared to baseline?
• Is baseline deviation reported?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Configuration Items (CIs) are components needing management - hardware, software, docs
CMDB stores CIs with attributes, relationships, and change history
CM process: Identify → Control → Status Accounting → Audit
Version control tracks code changes with history, branching, and rollback capability
CMDB accuracy must be verified through periodic audits comparing actual to recorded state

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following BEST describes the primary purpose of establishing a configuration baseline?
options: To document all known vulnerabilities in a system.
To provide a reference point for future changes and ensure deviations are properly managed.
To automatically remediate any unauthorized changes to system configurations.
To grant all users administrator privileges for maximum flexibility.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A configuration baseline represents an approved state of a configuration item at a specific point in time. It serves as a reference point for managing changes and ensuring deviations are properly controlled and authorized. The other options are incorrect because they do not accurately describe the primary purpose of a configuration baseline.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what a baseline represents in terms of a starting point or standard.

reveal.question: List the four main stages of the Configuration Management Process.
reveal.answer:
1. Identification
2. Control
3. Status Accounting
4. Verification and Audit

rows.label: Definition
values: Any component that needs to be managed and controlled within an organization's IT environment.
An approved and documented state of a CI at a specific point in time, serving as a reference for future changes.

rows.label: Purpose
values: To identify and track all components relevant to IT service delivery.
To provide a stable and known state for comparison, enabling effective change management and risk assessment.

rows.label: Example
values: A server, a software application, a network device, or a policy document.
The documented configuration of a server after applying security patches and hardening measures.
